subject: ap-precalculus
title: Exponential Decay: Medication Modeling, Competing Models & Semi-Log Linearization
stimulus:
A researcher is studying the elimination of a medication from the bloodstream. A patient receives a single dose and the amount of medication remaining is measured at several times after administration:

| Time $t$ (hours) | Amount $Q$ (mg) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 400 |
| 3 | 263 |
| 7 | 152 |
| 12 | 75 |
| 18 | 29 |

The researcher proposes the exponential decay model $Q(t) = 400 \cdot b^t$ for the amount of medication remaining, in milligrams, at time $t$ hours.

parts.prompt:
(i) Use the data at $t = 0$ and $t = 18$ to find the value of $b$ as a decimal approximation. Show the computations that lead to your answer.

(ii) Use the model to determine the half-life of the medication — the time at which the amount remaining equals half the initial dose. Express your answer as a decimal approximation. Show the computations that lead to your answer.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Correct value of $b$ — from $400 \cdot b^{18} = 29$: $b^{18} = \frac{29}{400} = 0.0725 \Rightarrow b = (0.0725)^{1/18} = e^{\ln(0.0725)/18} \approx e^{-2.624/18} \approx e^{-0.1458} \approx 0.8643$ (accept values in $[0.862, 0.867]$); supporting work must show the root-taking or logarithm step

1 pt: Correct half-life — solving $400 \cdot b^t = 200 \Rightarrow b^t = 0.5 \Rightarrow t = \frac{\ln(0.5)}{\ln(b)} = \frac{-0.6931}{\ln(0.8643)} \approx \frac{-0.6931}{-0.1458} \approx 4.753$ hours (accept values in $[4.7, 4.8]$); supporting work must show the logarithm step
parts.scoring_notes: For part (i), $\ln(29/400) = \ln(0.0725) \approx -2.624$, so $\ln b = -2.624/18 \approx -0.1458$, giving $b \approx 0.864$. For part (ii), $t_{1/2} = \ln(0.5)/\ln(b) = (-0.6931)/(-0.1458) \approx 4.75$ hours. Note: students who use a different data pair from the table (e.g., $t=3$ and $t=18$) will get a slightly different $b$; award credit for correct algebra regardless. A student who writes $t = \frac{\ln 2}{-\ln b}$ earns credit for the logarithm step.

parts.prompt:
(i) To determine whether an exponential model is more appropriate than a linear model for this data, create a semi-log linearization by computing $\ln(Q)$ for each data point. Using the values at $t = 0$ and $t = 18$, find the slope and $y$-intercept of the line on the semi-log plot of $\ln(Q)$ versus $t$.

(ii) The researcher observes that the five computed $\ln(Q)$ values — at $t = 0, 3, 7, 12, 18$ — all lie very close to the line found in part (i). Explain what this near-linearity implies about the appropriateness of the exponential model. Then identify the value $\ln(Q)$ at $t = 7$ and verify it is consistent with the line from part (i).
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Correct slope and intercept — slope $= \frac{\ln(29) - \ln(400)}{18 - 0} = \frac{3.367 - 5.991}{18} = \frac{-2.624}{18} \approx -0.1458$ (accept values in $[-0.147, -0.145]$) and $y$-intercept $= \ln(400) \approx 5.991$ (accept $[5.99, 5.99]$); supporting work must show the slope as a quotient of $\ln$ values

1 pt: Two-part response — (a) near-linearity on the semi-log plot confirms the exponential model is appropriate because if $Q = 400b^t$, then $\ln Q = \ln 400 + t\ln b$, which is linear in $t$; AND (b) $\ln(Q(7)) = \ln(152) \approx 5.024$; from the line: $5.991 + (-0.1458)(7) = 5.991 - 1.021 = 4.970$; these values are close (difference $\approx 0.054$), consistent with near-linearity (accept any correct computation showing $\ln(152)$ compared to the predicted line value)
parts.scoring_notes: For part (i), slope $= (\ln 29 - \ln 400)/18 \approx -2.624/18 \approx -0.1458$ and $y$-intercept $= \ln(400) \approx 5.991$. For part (ii), the key step is showing $\ln(ab^t)$ is linear in $t$. The verification at $t = 7$: $\ln(152) \approx 5.023$ vs. predicted $5.991 - 0.1458(7) = 4.970$; the small discrepancy (0.05) confirms approximate linearity. Accept any correct computation; students should note the residual is small, not zero, which reflects real data noise.

parts.prompt:
(i) A hospital protocol requires a second dose to be administered when the amount of medication in the patient's bloodstream falls below $50$ milligrams. Using the model $Q(t) = 400 \cdot b^t$ with $b$ from part (a), write and solve an inequality to find the earliest time $t$ at which a second dose should be given. Express your answer as a decimal approximation.

(ii) A colleague suggests using a linear model $L(t) = 400 - 20.6t$ to approximate $Q(t)$ for $t \geq 0$. Determine whether $L(t)$ overestimates or underestimates $Q(t)$ for all $t > 0$, and justify your answer using the concavity of $Q$.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Correct inequality and solution — $400 \cdot b^t < 50 \Rightarrow b^t < \frac{1}{8} \Rightarrow t > \frac{\ln(1/8)}{\ln(b)} = \frac{-\ln 8}{\ln(0.8643)} \approx \frac{-2.079}{-0.1458} \approx 14.26$ hours (accept values in $[14.1, 14.5]$); supporting work must show the inequality setup with direction preserved when dividing by negative $\ln(b)$, and the logarithm step

1 pt: Correct conclusion '$L$ overestimates $Q$ for $t > 0$ in the clinically relevant domain' with justification — $L(0) = Q(0) = 400$, but $L'(t) = -20.6$ while $Q'(0) = 400\ln(b) \approx -58.3$. Since $Q$ initially decreases much faster than $L$ (slope $-58.3$ vs $-20.6$), $L(t) > Q(t)$ for all $t > 0$ in the relevant range. Although $Q$ is concave up ($Q'' > 0$), $L$ is not tangent to $Q$ at $t = 0$ — it has a much gentler slope, so it lies above the curve, overestimating the medication amount
parts.scoring_notes: For part (i), $\ln(1/8)/\ln(0.8643) = (-2.079)/(-0.1458) \approx 14.26$ hours. The inequality direction flips when dividing by $\ln(b) < 0$; the rubric checks for this. For part (ii), the expected answer is 'overestimates.' The linear model $L(t) = 400 - 20.6t$ has slope $-20.6$, but $Q'(0) = 400\ln(0.8643) \approx -58.3$. Since $L$ decreases more slowly than $Q$ initially, $L(t) > Q(t)$ for $t > 0$. Award the point for: (a) correct conclusion ('overestimates'), (b) comparison of slopes at $t = 0$, and (c) explaining that $L$ falls more slowly than $Q$. Note: for very large $t$ (past $\approx 19$ hours), $L$ becomes negative while $Q$ stays positive, so technically $L$ eventually underestimates — but this is outside the clinically relevant domain.
